meta.note: Deep cuts: obscure history, chart lore, marine hazards, insider knowledge. Coordinates approximate. 'confidence' distinguishes documented facts from local lore/secondhand accounts. Generated by Claude, curated 2026-07-05.

items.name: Gorge Harbour Pictographs
items.description: Red-ochre pictographs on the west cliff of the harbour entrance, visible from the water as you pass through the gorge. Best viewed slowly by kayak at mid-tide.

items.name: Gorge Entrance Burial Caves
items.description: Ancient burial caves reported on the east side of the gorge entrance in cruising guides. Culturally sensitive; not a visitation site.

items.name: Yi'piikwu and Sa'y'ilh
items.description: Klahoose names for the outside and inside of Gorge Harbour. Sa'y'ilh means 'two waters in one' — saltwater mixing with spring and stream freshwater. Oral tradition holds the entrance froze in winter and canoes broke through the ice.

items.name: The Salty Stream of Squirrel Cove
items.description: In July 1792 Broughton and Menzies of the Vancouver expedition landed at a stream mouth in Squirrel Cove and were baffled to find the stream salty — tidal backflow from the lagoon. They shot grouse but couldn't get near the deer.

items.name: Named for a Man Who Never Came
items.description: Hernán Cortés never came within thousands of kilometres of this coast and had been dead ~245 years when Galiano and Valdés attached his name to the island in 1792.

items.name: Marina Island Is La Malinche
items.description: Marina Island is named for Doña Marina (La Malinche), Cortés's Nahua interpreter and mistress. British charts renamed it 'Mary Island' in the 19th century; the original name was restored in 1906.

items.name: The Subtle Islands Pun
items.description: Camp Island off Cortes was renamed the Subtle Islands — an English pun on Sutil, the Spanish schooner ('subtle' is what sutil means). Chart-name humour hiding in plain sight.

items.name: Chart Name Musical Chairs
items.description: Local points swapped names repeatedly after the 1860 survey: Mary Island became Marina, Turn Point became Mary Point (Vancouver's original 1792 name, possibly for his sister), and Reef Point became Sutil Point.

items.name: 'Open Prairie Land' Notation
items.description: The 1860 Admiralty survey marked south Cortes and Marina Island as 'open prairie land' — likely Indigenous-maintained camas meadows and village clearings, not wild grassland.

items.name: Cor-TEEZ
items.description: Pioneers spelled it Cortez and pronounced it 'Cor-teez'. Old-timers and their descendants still do; the pronunciation instantly dates a family's arrival on the island.

items.name: Whaling Station That Emptied the Sea
items.description: The Dawson & Douglass whaling station at Whaletown operated only about a year (1869–70) but effectively wiped out local whale populations — an ecological absence that lasted nearly 150 years until the humpback return.

items.name: Smelt Bay Village Site
items.description: Smelt Bay is an old Indigenous village site; the beach ridges behind it are cultural features. Paddlers still treat it as a water stop, mostly unaware of what they're standing on.

items.name: Smallpox Abandonment at Sunny Brae
items.description: John Manson's 1888 'Sunny Brae' homestead had an already-cleared shoreline — an abandoned Indigenous summer camp, with artifacts and middens dug up for decades after. The 1862–63 smallpox epidemic, which killed as much as two-thirds of the regional population, likely explains the abandonment.

items.name: Mutton Pickers of Mitlenatch
items.description: The Manson family ran sheep and cattle on Mitlenatch Island, butchering cattle there and rowing the meat to Comox. In 1892 they lived on the island in a driftwood cabin specifically to deter 'mutton pickers' — sheep rustlers.

items.name: Cactus and Giant Snakes
items.description: Mitlenatch sits in the Vancouver Island rain shadow and has a semi-desert microclimate: brittle prickly-pear cactus blooms in late June, and western terrestrial garter snakes grow past 90 cm and hunt sculpins in the sea.

items.name: Mitlenatch's Name
items.description: Mitlenatch is an English mangling of a Tla'amin term often glossed as 'calm waters all around' — not German, despite how it sounds.

items.name: Manson Died in Office, Mid-Journey
items.description: Michael Manson — trader, steamship owner, justice of the peace, MLA — died in 1932 in Bella Coola while travelling as a sitting member of the legislature. His widow Jane returned to Hernando Island.

items.name: The Thistle and the Stella
items.description: Michael Manson owned and operated his own vessels: the steamship Thistle and the steam tug Stella. Logs from 1891 and 1895 survive in the museum archives.

items.name: Clytosin
items.description: Manson's Landing was known as Clytosin when Michael Manson pre-empted there — the settler name overwrote an existing one within a generation.

items.name: 1908 Orchard Still Producing
items.description: Sunny Brae Farm's first orchard was planted in 1908 and many of those trees still bear fruit. The farm earned a BC Century Farm Award in 2013 — over a century of continuous family farming, now organic blueberries.

items.name: Ghost Orchards
items.description: Twisted fruit trees in long-abandoned clearings mark failed homesteads across the island — the population peaked in the 1920s, then collapsed under isolation, depression, and freight costs. Finding an unfamiliar apple tree deep in second-growth is a Cortes rite of passage.

items.name: Japanese Horse Loggers
items.description: Japanese logging crews worked Cortes with horses in the early settlement era — a largely unrecorded chapter that predates the homestead wave.

items.name: Electricity Arrived in 1970
items.description: BC Hydro power reached Cortes only in 1970, a year after the first car ferry (the 16-car Cortes Queen, 1969). Anyone over ~60 who grew up on-island remembers lamplight.

items.name: Peggy Newsham, Queen of Cortes
items.description: Smelt Bay matriarch under five feet tall who reportedly held a driver's licence valid only on Cortes Island. Locals pulled over when they saw her VW Bug coming.

items.name: The Weilers' Half-Built House
items.description: Mary and Ottie Weiler chartered a boat up the coast in April 1947 seeking a 'happy, romantic, Bohemian life,' sailed into Whaletown Bay, saw a half-built house on the beach, and stayed. The family shaped Whaletown's artistic culture for generations.

items.name: Museum Building Is the Old Store
items.description: The Cortes Museum occupies the small general store built on Manson's 1880s trading-post site — the building itself was moved in 1995 from the landing to its current spot on Beasley Road.

items.name: 4,000 Photos Online
items.description: The Cortes Museum archives have digitized 4,000+ historical photographs, searchable online — an unusually deep visual record for an island of 1,000 people.

items.name: The Free Store
items.description: The Cortes recycling centre includes a free store that functions as the island's informal redistribution economy. Locals check it before buying anything; the best scores are never mentioned publicly.

items.name: Squirrel Cove Reversing Rapids
items.description: The tidal entrance to Squirrel Cove's inner lagoon runs as a small reversing rapid. On a flooding tide you can ride it in by kayak or dinghy; timing the exit wrong means waiting hours.

items.name: Uganda Passage Dogleg
items.description: The channel between Whaletown and Shark Spit is a narrow dredged dogleg between markers; run it wrong and you're aground on sand at 3 knots. Locals treat it as a competence filter for visiting boats.

items.name: Sutil Point Boulder Field
items.description: The reef off Sutil Point extends far offshore; the Q20 buoy marking it sits so far out that skippers routinely fail to find it. The stretch from Sutil to Savary at extreme low tide becomes a boulder field, one harbour seal per rock.

items.name: Von Donop Entrance Rock
items.description: A dangerous rock lies mid-channel partway along the Von Donop Inlet entrance. Cruising guides say tick-over speed, bow watch, favour the north side. It has caught boats for a century.

items.name: Gorge Harbour False Bottom
items.description: Most of Gorge Harbour is shale bottom where anchors will not bite; the only reliable holding is the northwest corner. Uncharted rocks lie around the Tan, Ring, Pill and Stove islets. Every season someone learns this at 2 a.m.

items.name: Klahoose Bought the Gorge
items.description: Gorge Harbour Marina Resort is now 100% owned by the Klahoose First Nation — the island's main resort operating on the Nation's own traditional harbour.

items.name: Toba Flood Relocation
items.description: The Klahoose settled permanently at Squirrel Cove in the late 1890s after flooding at their Toba Inlet village — Squirrel Cove had long been a spring/summer harvesting place with planted gardens.

items.name: Hague Lake's Ancient Trout
items.description: Hague Lake holds a resident cutthroat trout population locals describe as an ancient strain; motorboats are banned and the lake stays swimmable-clear as a protected drinking watershed.

items.name: Seasonal Coast Guard Station
items.description: A Canadian Coast Guard Inshore Rescue Boat station operates out of Cortes Bay in summer only — one of the few seasonal IRB posts on the coast.

items.name: The Rain Shadow
items.description: Cortes sits in the Vancouver Island rain shadow: measurably drier and sunnier than Campbell River across the channel. The gradient is steep enough that Mitlenatch, 12 km south, grows cactus.

items.name: Wolves Swim the Channels
items.description: Coastal wolves periodically swim to Cortes from adjacent islands and den here; Von Donop trail advisories warn hikers. The island cycles between wolf-present and wolf-absent years.

items.name: Linnaea School's Ghost
items.description: The alternative Linnaea School operated at the farm until 2010, closing for lack of students and funding. After grade 9, island kids still face the choice: homeschool, boarding, or the ferry commute.

items.name: Craftshop Co-op
items.description: The Cortes Craftshop at Squirrel Cove is run cooperatively by 30+ artists who take turns staffing it — no employees, pure rotation, operating this way for decades.

items.name: No Confirmed Shipwrecks
items.description: Despite the hazards (Sutil reef, Uganda Passage, Von Donop rock, Gorge shale), no major named shipwreck on Cortes shores is well documented in public sources — local strandings and small-boat losses went largely unrecorded. The museum archives are the place to dig.
